en.extract:
The edible-nest swiftlet (Aerodramus fuciphagus), also known as the white-nest swiftlet, is a small bird of the swift family which is found in Southeast Asia. Its opaque and whitish nest is made exclusively of solidified saliva and is the main ingredient of bird's nest soup, a delicacy of Chinese cuisine. Germain's swiftlet (Aerodramus germani) is now treated as conspecific with this species.


Taxonomy
The edible-nest swiftlet was formally described in 1812 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Peter Thunberg under the binomial name Hirundo fuciphaga based on a specimen collected on the island of Java. The specific epithet fuciphaga combines the Ancient Greek φυκος/phukos meaning "seaweed" with -φαγος/-phagos meaning "-eating". The edible-nest swiftlet is now one of 25 swiftlets placed in the genus Aerodramus that was introduced in 1906 by the American ornithologist Harry C. Oberholser.
Eight subspecies are recognised:

A. f. inexpectatus (Hume, 1873) – Andaman and Nicobar Islands
A. f. vestitus (Lesson, RP, 1843) – Malay Peninsula (?introduced), Sumatra, Belitung (east of south Sumatra) and Borneo
A. f. perplexus (Riley, 1927) – Maratua (east of northeast Borneo)
A. f. fuciphagus (Thunberg, 1812) – Java, Kangean Islands (north of Bali), Bali, Lombok to Sumbawa (west Lesser Sunda Islands, Flores Sea islands south of Sulawesi)
A. f. dammermani (Rensch, 1931) – Flores to Alor Island (central Lesser Sunda Islands)
A. f. micans (Stresemann, 1914) – Sumba, Savu and Timor (central south Lesser Sunda Islands)
A. f. germani (Oustalet, 1876) – southeast Asia, north Borneo islets, Panay and Palawan group (central west, southwest Philippines)
A. f. amechanus (Oberholser, 1912) – Anambas (east of southeast Malay Peninsula)
The last two subspecies in the above list (germani and amechanus) have sometimes been treated as a separate species, Germain's swiftlet (Aerodramus germani). The species are lumped together as the morphological differences are minor and clinal while the genetic differences are small.


Description

The edible-nest swiftlet, generally with a body length of 14 cm (5.5 in), is a medium-sized representative of the salanganes. The upper part of the slender body is blackish-brown; the under part of the body ranges in colour from white to blackish-brown. The tail is short and has a slight notch. The bill and feet are black. Legs are very short and tarsi are usually unfeathered or lightly feathered. 
It weighs 15 to 18 g (0.53 to 0.63 oz) and the wings are long and narrow. In flight the swept-back wings resemble a crescent. 
The subspecies micans is paler and greyer than the nominate while vestitus is dark with a rump that is less obviously paler. Subspecies germani has much paler underparts with a broad whitish rump, amechanus is similar to germani but has a greyer rump.


Behavior

The edible-nest swiftlet feeds over a range of habitats from coastal areas to the mountains, occurring up to 2,800 metres above sea-level on Sumatra and Borneo. These birds generally occur above forests, the forest edge, but also in open country.
These birds spend most of their lives in the air. Their diet consists of flying insects that are caught on the wing. They also drink on the wing. They often feed in large flocks with other species of swiftlet and swallow.
They breed in colonies in coastal areas, in limestone caves, in rock crevices, in a cleft in a cliff or sometimes on a building. The bracket-shaped nest is built on a vertical surface and the long legs are used for clinging. These swifts never settle voluntarily on the ground. The nest is white and translucent and is made of layers of hardened saliva attached to the rock. 
A nest typically measures about 6 cm across with a depth of 1.5 cm and a weight of about 14 grams. Two white, oval, non-glossy eggs are laid.
At breeding colonies, the birds emit high-pitched and burbling calls. They also emit a rattling call used for echolocation, which enables them to look for their nesting sites in the darkness of caves.


Threats and conservation

The nest used in bird's nest soup is composed entirely of saliva. The soup is made by soaking and steaming the nests in water. It is said to improve kidney health, reduce phlegm, and to be an aphrodisiac. The nests can fetch high prices and many colonies are harvested commercially.
Some populations such as those in the  Andaman and Nicobar Islands have been harvested extensively leading to them being considered critically threatened under the IUCN criteria.
The use of artificial bird houses is growing. A detailed account of modern nest farming and marketing techniques is given by David Jordan (2004).
In Indonesia and Malaysia, "farming" of nests is performed in purpose-built structures or old empty houses with "tweeters" playing recordings of bird calls on the roof to attract swiftlets. In urban areas, such "bird houses" may be considered a nuisance by neighbours due to the loud bird calls and bird feces.


References


Further reading
MacKinnon, John & Phillipps, Karen (1993) A Field Guide to the Birds of Borneo, Sumatra, Java and Bali, Oxford University Press, Oxford.
Manchi, Shirish S.; Sankaran, Ravi (2010). Foraging habits and habitat use by edible nest and glossy swiftlets in the Andsman Islands The Wilson Journal of Ornithology. 122 (2): 259–272. ISSN 1559-4491.
Robson, Craig (2002) A Field Guide to the Birds of South-East Asia, New Holland Publishers (UK) Ltd., London.


External links
 Data related to Aerodramus fuciphagus at Wikispecies
 Media related to Aerodramus fuciphagus at Wikimedia Commons
Oriental Bird Images: Edible-nest Swiftlet
Review of Scientific Research on Edible Bird's Nest

The '''edible-nest swiftlet''' ('''''Aerodramus fuciphagus'''''), also known as the '''white-nest swiftlet''', is a small [[bird]] of the [[Swift (bird)|swift]] family which is found in [[Southeast Asia]]. Its opaque and whitish [[nest]] is made exclusively of solidified [[saliva]] and is the main ingredient of [[bird's nest soup]], a delicacy of Chinese cuisine. Germain's swiftlet (''Aerodramus germani'') is now treated as [[conspecific]] with this species.

==Taxonomy==
The edible-nest swiftlet was [[species description|formally described]] in 1812 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Peter Thunberg]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Hirundo fuciphaga'' based on a specimen collected on the island of Java.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Thunberg | first=Carl Peter | author-link=Carl Peter Thunberg | date=1812 | title=Anmärkningar om de Svalor, som bygga gelé-acktige ätbare nästen | language=Swedish, Latin | journal=Kungl. Svenska Vetenskapsakademiens Handlingar | volume=33 | pages=151–156 [153–154] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47088097 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=222 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476693 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''fuciphaga'' combines the [[Ancient Greek]] {{lang|grc|φυκος}}/''{{transliteration|grc|phukos}}'' meaning "seaweed" with {{lang|grc|-φαγος}}/''{{transliteration|grc|-phagos}}'' meaning "-eating".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=fuciphaga | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=fuciphaga | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=9 November 2024 }}</ref> The edible-nest swiftlet is now one of 25 swiftlets placed in the genus ''[[Aerodramus]]'' that was introduced in 1906 by the American ornithologist [[Harry C. Oberholser]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Owlet-nightjars, treeswifts & swifts | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/swifts/  | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 November 2024}}</ref>
 
Eight [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''A. f. inexpectatus'' ([[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1873) – [[Andaman Islands|Andaman]] and [[Nicobar Islands]]
* ''A. f. vestitus'' ([[René Lesson|Lesson, RP]], 1843) – [[Malay Peninsula]] (?introduced), [[Sumatra]], [[Belitung]] (east of south [[Sumatra]]) and [[Borneo]]
* ''A. f. perplexus'' ([[Joseph Harvey Riley|Riley]], 1927) – [[Derawan Islands|Maratua]] (east of northeast [[Borneo]])
* ''A. f. fuciphagus'' ([[Carl Peter Thunberg|Thunberg]], 1812) – [[Java]], [[Kangean Islands]] (north of [[Bali]]), [[Bali]], [[Lombok]] to [[Sumbawa]] (west [[Lesser Sunda Islands]], [[Flores Sea]] islands south of [[Sulawesi]])
* ''A. f. dammermani'' (Rensch, 1931) – [[Flores]] to [[Alor Island]] (central [[Lesser Sunda Islands]])
* ''A. f. micans'' ([[Erwin Stresemann|Stresemann]], 1914) – [[Sumba]], [[Savu]] and [[Timor]] (central south [[Lesser Sunda Islands]])
* ''A. f. germani'' ([[Émile Oustalet|Oustalet]], 1876) – southeast Asia, north [[Borneo]] islets, [[Panay]] and [[Palawan]] group (central west, southwest Philippines)
* ''A. f. amechanus'' ([[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1912) – [[Anambas Islands Regency|Anambas]] (east of southeast [[Malay Peninsula]])
The last two subspecies in the above list (''germani'' and ''amechanus'') have sometimes been treated as a separate species, Germain's swiftlet (''Aerodramus germani''). The species are lumped together as the morphological differences are minor and clinal while the genetic differences are small.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{ cite web | last1=Clements | first1=J.F.| author1-link=James Clements (ornithologist) | last2=Rasmussen | first2=P.C. | author2-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | last3=Schulenberg | first3=T.S. | last4=Iliff | first4=M.J. | last5=Fredericks | first5=T.A. | last6=Gerbracht | first6=J.A. | last7=Lepage | first7=D. | last8=Spencer | first8=A. | last9=Billerman | first9=S.M. | last10=Sullivan | first10=B.L. | last11=Smith | first11=M. | last12=Wood | first12=C.L. | year=2024 | title=The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2024 | url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ | access-date=26 October 2024 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Cibois | first1=A. | last2=Thibault | first2=J.-C. | last3=McCormack | first3=G. | last4=Pasquet | first4=E. | date=2018 | title=Phylogenetic relationships of the Eastern Polynesian swiftlets (''Aerodramus'', Apodidae) and considerations on other Western Pacific swiftlets | journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology | volume=118 | issue=3 | pages=247–257 | doi=10.1080/01584197.2017.1420422| bibcode=2018EmuAO.118..247C }}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Cranbrook | first1=Earl of | last2=Goh | first2=W.L. | last3=Lim | first3=C.K. | last4=Mustafa | first4=A.R. | date=2013 | title=The species of white-nest swiftlets (Apodidae, Collocaliini) of Malaysia and the origins of house-farm birds: morphometric and genetic evidence | journal=Forktail | volume=29 | pages=107–119 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/58109384 }}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File: Edible-nest Swiftlet Aerodramus fuciphagus nest best by Raju Kasambe 02.jpg|thumb|left|Swiftlets nesting in a building in the Andaman Islands]]
The edible-nest swiftlet, generally with a body length of {{cvt|14|cm}}, is a medium-sized representative of the salanganes.<ref name=Chantler>{{ cite book | last1=Chantler | first1=Phil | last2=Driessens | first2=Gerald | date=2000 | title=Swifts: A Guide to the Swifts and Treeswifts of the World | edition=2nd | location=New Haven | publisher=Yale University Press | isbn=0-300-07936-2 | pages=150–152 | url=https://archive.org/details/swiftsguidetoswi0000chan/page/150/mode/1up | url-access=registration }}</ref> The upper part of the slender body is blackish-brown; the under part of the body ranges in colour from white to blackish-brown. The tail is short and has a slight notch.<ref name=India/> The [[Beak|bill]] and feet are black. Legs are very short and [[Tarsometatarsus|tarsi]] are usually unfeathered or lightly feathered.<ref name=Guide> Allen Jeyarajasingam [https://books.google.com/books?id=XCwUDAAAQBAJ&dq=%22edible+nest+swiftlet%22&pg=PA224 A Field Guide to the Birds of Peninsular Malaysia and Singapore] Oxford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-19-963942-7}}</ref> 

It weighs {{cvt|15|to|18|g}}<ref> C. M. Francis: The management of edible birds nest caves in Sabah. Sabah Forestry Department, Sandakan 1987</ref> and the wings are long and narrow. In flight the swept-back wings resemble a crescent.<ref name=India/> 

The [[subspecies]] ''micans'' is paler and greyer than the [[nominate subspecies|nominate]] while ''vestitus'' is dark with a rump that is less obviously paler. Subspecies ''germani'' has much paler underparts with a broad whitish rump, ''amechanus'' is similar to ''germani'' but has a greyer rump.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Chantler | first=P. | year=1999 | chapter=Family Apodidae (Swifts) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=5: Barn-owls to Hummingbirds | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-25-2 | pages=388–457 [434] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0005unse/page/434/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Behavior==
[[File: AerodramusFuciphagus.LipKee.jpg|thumb|left|In flight]]
The edible-nest swiftlet feeds over a range of habitats from coastal areas to the mountains, occurring up to 2,800 metres above sea-level on Sumatra and Borneo. These birds generally occur above forests, the forest edge, but also in open country.<ref name=Guide/>

These birds spend most of their lives in the air. Their diet consists of flying [[insect]]s that are caught on the wing.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Lourie | first1=S.A. | last2=Tompkins | first2=D.M. | date=2000 | title=The diets of Malaysian swiftlets | journal=Ibis | volume=142 | issue=4 | pages=596–602 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2000.tb04459.x}}</ref> They also drink on the wing.<ref name=India/> They often feed in large flocks with other species of swiftlet and [[swallow]].<ref name=Guide/>

They breed in [[Bird colony|colonies]] in coastal areas, in limestone caves, in rock crevices, in a cleft in a cliff or sometimes on a building.<ref name=Guide/> The bracket-shaped [[nest]] is built on a vertical surface and the long legs are used for clinging. These swifts never settle voluntarily on the ground.<ref name = India>[https://www.beautyofbirds.com/indianswiftlets.html Indian Swiftlets or Indian Edible-nest Swiftlets]</ref> The nest is white and translucent and is made of layers of hardened saliva attached to the rock. 

A nest typically measures about 6 cm across with a depth of 1.5 cm and a weight of about 14 grams. Two white, oval, non-glossy [[egg (biology)|eggs]] are laid.<ref name=India/>

At breeding colonies, the birds emit high-pitched and burbling calls.<ref name=Guide/> They also emit a rattling call used for [[Animal echolocation|echolocation]], which enables them to look for their nesting sites in the darkness of caves.<ref name=Chantler/><ref name=Guide/><ref> Hendrik A. Thomassen: [https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Swift-as-sound.-Design-and-evolution-of-the-system-Thomassen/86e92a84b8094180912a957cb33fd2d59863a023  Swift as sound. Design and evolution of the echolocation system in Swiftlets (Apodidae: Collocaliini).] Universität Leiden, 2005 </ref>

==Threats and conservation==
[[File:SwiftletBirdNests.jpg|thumb|The nest before use in [[bird's nest soup]]]]
The nest used in bird's nest soup is composed entirely of saliva. The soup is made by soaking and steaming the nests in water. It is said to improve kidney health, reduce phlegm, and to be an [[aphrodisiac]].<ref name=India/><ref>{{Cite web|date=2017-10-23| title=Bird's nest soup is more popular than ever, thanks to Swiftlet House farms|url=https://www.audubon.org/news/birds-nest-soup-more-popular-ever-thanks-swiftlet-house-farms|access-date=2020-06-29|website=Audubon|language=en}}</ref> The nests can fetch high prices and many colonies are harvested commercially.

Some populations such as those in the  [[Andaman Islands|Andaman]] and [[Nicobar Islands]] have been harvested extensively leading to them being considered critically threatened under the IUCN criteria.<ref name=iucn/><ref>{{cite journal |last=Sankaran | first=R. |year=2001 |title=The status and conservation of the edible-nest swiftlet (''Collocalia fuciphaga'') in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands | journal=Biological Conservation | volume=97 | issue=3 | pages=283–294 | doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(00)00124-5 | bibcode=2001BCons..97..283S }}</ref>

The use of artificial bird houses is growing.<ref>{{cite web|last=Boyle|first=Joe|title=Welcome to Indonesia's bird nest soup factory town|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-pacific-12274825|publisher=BBC|date=2011}}</ref> A detailed account of modern nest farming and marketing techniques is given by David Jordan (2004).<ref name = global> Jordan, David, 2004 [https://web.archive.org/web/20081021051925/http://jordanresearch.co.uk/pubs.html "Globalisation and Bird's Nest Soup"] International Development Planning Review, Volume 26, Number 1, Liverpool Unviversity Press 2004</ref>

In [[Indonesia]] and [[Malaysia]], "farming" of nests is performed in purpose-built structures or old empty houses with "[[Tweeter|tweeters]]" playing recordings of bird calls on the roof to attract swiftlets. In urban areas, such "bird houses" may be considered a nuisance by neighbours due to the loud bird calls and bird feces.<ref name=global/>

==References==
{{Reflist}}

==Further reading==
* MacKinnon, John & Phillipps, Karen (1993) ''A Field Guide to the Birds of Borneo, Sumatra, Java and Bali'', Oxford University Press, Oxford.
* Manchi, Shirish S.; Sankaran, Ravi (2010). [https://www.jstor.org/stable/40600416 Foraging habits and habitat use by edible nest and glossy swiftlets in the Andsman Islands] The Wilson Journal of Ornithology. 122 (2): 259–272. ISSN 1559-4491.
* Robson, Craig (2002) ''A Field Guide to the Birds of South-East Asia'', New Holland Publishers (UK) Ltd., London.

==External links==

*{{Commons category-inline|Aerodramus fuciphagus}}

zh.extract:
爪哇金丝燕，学名Aerodramus fuciphagus，雨燕目雨燕科的一种。


分布区域
爪哇金丝燕分布於东南亚沿海地區，安達曼群島，印尼島嶼，以及中國海南島等地。


外部特征
上身黑褐色，腰部有淡色腰斑，下身灰褐色。


习性
此鸟终日沿着海岸、岛屿飞行捕食飞虫，几乎很少休息。飞行时不似家燕那样作急剧转折，这是因为它的尾羽不呈叉状的缘故。繁殖期在4~6月，每窝产两枚卵。它的巢为半透明塑胶碟状，通常筑于崖隙或岩洞中，采制为名貴食品燕窝。


参考文献
